request.question: Who was born first Aretha Franklin or Andy Yorke?

title: The Electrifying Aretha Franklin
Soft and Beautiful
(You Make Me Feel Like) A Natural Woman
Yeah!!!
Andy Yorke
Songs of Faith (Aretha Franklin album)
Aretha: With The Ray Bryant Combo
Aretha Franklin
Hey Now Hey (The Other Side of the Sky)
Laughing on the Outside
sentences: The Electrifying Aretha Franklin is the third studio album by American singer Aretha Franklin, Released on March 19, 1962 by Columbia Records.
The album which also known under its working title "The Incomparable Aretha Franklin", was recorded at Columbia Recording Studios, 799 Seventh Avenue, New York.
It was produced by John Hammond and arranged by Richard Wess.
Soft and Beautiful is the seventeenth studio album by the American singer Aretha Franklin, released on June 20, 1969, by Columbia Records.

The album was recorded when Franklin was 22 years old between July 9 and 16, 1964, It was her last album on Columbia before she moved to Atlantic Records and until 1969, unreleased, although an alternative version of "A Mother's Love" appeared on Franklin's 1966 Columbia LP "Soul Sister".
It reached Number 29 on "Billboard"'s R&B chart.
Mark Bego, in "Aretha Franklin: The Queen of Soul", called it "the most consistently paced album of her later Columbia years".
Originally released by Columbia Records, the album was reissued on CD with her 1962 album "The Tender, The Moving, The Swinging Aretha Franklin".
"(You Make Me Feel Like) A Natural Woman" is a 1967 single released by American soul singer Aretha Franklin on the Atlantic label.
The song was co-written by Carole King and Gerry Goffin, with input from Atlantic producer Jerry Wexler.
The record was a big hit for Franklin, reaching number 8 on the "Billboard" Hot 100, and became a standard song for her.
Franklin also included a live recording on the album "Aretha in Paris" in 1968.
Versions have also been performed and recorded by King herself, Mary J. Blige, Celine Dion, and many others.
At the 2015 Kennedy Center Honors, Aretha Franklin performed the song to honor award-recipient Carole King.
Yeah!!!
(or Aretha Franklin In Person With Her Quartet) is the eighth studio album by American singer Aretha Franklin, Released on May 17, 1965 by Columbia Records.
Contrary to the overdubbed sounds of audience murmurs, the album was not a live album, but instead was recorded live at New York's Columbia Studios and produced by Clyde Otis.
This would be Franklin's last collection of jazz recordings until the release of 1969's "Soul '69", released during her landmark tenure at Atlantic Records.

An expanded version of the album that also contains the original session tracks without audience overdubs has been released on CD in the Columbia Box Set "Take A Look: Aretha Franklin Complete On Columbia".
Andy Yorke (born 10 January 1972) is an English musician and former lead singer and guitarist for the band Unbelievable Truth.
He is the younger brother of Radiohead frontman Thom Yorke.
Songs of Faith is the debut studio album by American singer Aretha Franklin, Released in 1956 by JVB/Battle Records.
The album was recorded live when Franklin was aged 14 at New Bethel Baptist Church, the church of her father Reverend C. L. Franklin.
The album was originally issued on JVB LP 100 and Battle LP 6105.
It is always known on Checker Records as Checker LPS-10009.
Songs of Faith has been reissued many times under various names.
It is known also as "The Gospel Soul of Aretha Franklin", "Aretha's Gospel", "Precious Lord", "You Grow Closer", "Never Grow Old", and "The First Album".
Aretha: With The Ray Bryant Combo is the second studio album by American singer songwriter, Aretha Franklin, released on February 27, 1961 by Columbia Records.
The album is Aretha's first release for Columbia, and is also known under its working title "Right Now It's Aretha" and sometimes simply as "Aretha".

Following in the footsteps of her close friend Sam Cooke, Aretha was "discovered" by famed Columbia Records producer John H. Hammond, who on the cover notes of the 1973 edition of "The Great Aretha Franklin: The First 12 Sides" mentions, that she was in fact recommended by the composer Curtis Reginald Lewis.
With the support of her father, Reverend C.L. Franklin, Aretha ventured out to New York City's Columbia Record Studios to record her debut album for the label.
Hammond paired Aretha Franklin with Ray Bryant, and combo and arranger J. Leslie McFarland, while taking charge of the album's production, which was received to mixed reviews.
Aretha Louise Franklin (born March 25, 1942) is an American singer and songwriter.
Franklin began her career as a child singing gospel at the church of her father, minister C. L. Franklin's church.
In 1960, at the age of 18, Franklin embarked on a secular career, recording for Columbia Records but only achieving modest success.

Following her signing to Atlantic Records in 1967, Franklin achieved commercial acclaim and success with songs such as "Respect", "(You Make Me Feel Like) A Natural Woman", "Spanish Harlem" and "Think".
By the end of the 1960s decade she had gained the title "The Queen of Soul".
Hey Now Hey (The Other Side of the Sky) is the twenty-first studio album by American singer-songwriter Aretha Franklin.
Released on June 25, 1973 by Atlantic Records, It was the first Atlantic album by Aretha to miss the Top 25 of the album chart.
(It peaked at #30.)

This album was originally planned to be a straight jazz album with songs like "Moody's Mood" and "Just Right Tonight", but producers Franklin and Quincy Jones took a detour and produced songs like "Mister Spain" and the title cut.
Critics dismissed this album as Aretha's worst LP up until that point.
The album is however considered a classic to some of Aretha's die-hard fans.

A high point was the #1 R&B and Top 20 Pop single "Angel" written by Aretha's younger sister, Carolyn Franklin, as well as her cover of "Somewhere" which features Franklin on piano and Phil Woods on alto sax.
This recording was reissued on compact disc through Rhino Records in the 1990s.
The song "Master Of Eyes" was included as the 10th track on the reissue.
Even though it was a single, it was not included on the first release.
Laughing on the Outside is the fifth studio album by American singer Aretha Franklin, Released on August 12, 1963 by Columbia Records.
The album was recorded at Columbia Recording Studios in New York and Hollywood.
These sessions found a 21-year-old Aretha Franklin recording Jazz Music and Pop Music standards, from Johnny Mercer to Duke Ellington.
She is backed by the arrangements of Columbia producer Robert Mersey.
One of the most popular songs from the album is Aretha's interpretation of the classic "Skylark".
A minute and fifty-eight seconds into the song, Aretha sings the word "Skylark" with power and emotion.
This was one of the first times in which Aretha recorded one of her written compositions, "I Wonder (Where Are You Tonight)", on an album.
Though somewhat overlooked in her Columbia catalogue, this album was jointly re-released with "The Electrifying Aretha Franklin" in June 2008.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Who was born first Aretha Franklin or Andy Yorke?
hop1_docs: Aretha: With The Ray Bryant Combo | Aretha: With The Ray Bryant Combo is the second studio album by American singer songwriter, Aretha Franklin, released on February 27, 1961 by Columbia Records.  The album is Aretha's first release for Columbia, and is also known under its working title "Right Now It's Aretha" and sometimes simply as "Aretha".  Following in the footsteps of her close friend Sam Cooke, Aretha was "discovered" by famed Columbia Records producer John H. Hammond, who on the cover notes of the 1973 edition of "The Great Aretha Franklin: The First 12 Sides" mentions, that she was in fact recommended by the composer Curtis Reginald Lewis.  With the support of her father, Reverend C.L. Franklin, Aretha ventured out to New York City's Columbia Record Studios to record her debut album for the label.  Hammond paired Aretha Franklin with Ray Bryant, and combo and arranger J. Leslie McFarland, while taking charge of the album's production, which was received to mixed reviews.

The Electrifying Aretha Franklin | The Electrifying Aretha Franklin is the third studio album by American singer Aretha Franklin, Released on March 19, 1962 by Columbia Records.  The album which also known under its working title "The Incomparable Aretha Franklin", was recorded at Columbia Recording Studios, 799 Seventh Avenue, New York.  It was produced by John Hammond and arranged by Richard Wess.

Laughing on the Outside | Laughing on the Outside is the fifth studio album by American singer Aretha Franklin, Released on August 12, 1963 by Columbia Records.  The album was recorded at Columbia Recording Studios in New York and Hollywood.  These sessions found a 21-year-old Aretha Franklin recording Jazz Music and Pop Music standards, from Johnny Mercer to Duke Ellington.  She is backed by the arrangements of Columbia producer Robert Mersey.  One of the most popular songs from the album is Aretha's interpretation of the classic "Skylark".  A minute and fifty-eight seconds into the song, Aretha sings the word "Skylark" with power and emotion.  This was one of the first times in which Aretha recorded one of her written compositions, "I Wonder (Where Are You Tonight)", on an album.  Though somewhat overlooked in her Columbia catalogue, this album was jointly re-released with "The Electrifying Aretha Franklin" in June 2008.

Aretha Franklin Sings the Great Diva Classics | Aretha Franklin Sings the Great Diva Classics is the forty-first studio album by American recording artist Aretha Franklin and was released on October 17, 2014.  It features ten covers of songs made famous by female recording artists.  This is Aretha's first recording for RCA Records and her first studio album to be released under a major label in 11 years - since the release of her 2003 studio album, "So Damn Happy".

Aretha Franklin discography | This discography documents the releases of albums and singles by Aretha Franklin, who is ranked first among female vocalists with the most "Billboard" chart hits during the rock era (1955–2012) with a total of 88 according to Joel Whitburn's Record Research.

Rewind: The Aretha Franklin Songbook | Rewind: The Aretha Franklin Songbook is the sixth studio and first tribute album by ARIA Award winning, Torres Strait Islander singer Christine Anu.  The album and tour were announced in April 2012 and released on 3 August 2012, to coincide with Aretha Franklin's 70th Birthday.

Who's Zoomin' Who? | Who's Zoomin' Who?  is the thirty-third studio album by American singer Aretha Franklin, originally released in the summer of 1985, on Arista Records.  It was Aretha's first-ever Platinum record and received praising critical reviews from people such as Robert Christgau.  The disc's first of five singles, "Freeway of Love", proved both a commercial hit as well as a career achievement for Franklin, earning her a Grammy Award for Best Female R&B Vocal Performance.
hop1_titles: Aretha: With The Ray Bryant Combo
Aretha Franklin Sings the Great Diva Classics
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Aretha Franklin (albums/age references); Andy Yorke (not mentioned / missing).

- Aretha Franklin factual spans in passages: album titles and release dates — "Aretha: With The Ray Bryant Combo" released "February 27, 1961"; "The Electrifying Aretha Franklin" released "March 19, 1962"; "Laughing on the Outside" released "August 12, 1963"; "Aretha Franklin Sings the Great Diva Classics" released "October 17, 2014"; "Who's Zoomin' Who?" released "summer of 1985".  
- Aretha age/birthday mentions: passages state "These sessions found a 21-year-old Aretha Franklin" (Laughing on the Outside, 1963) and a release was timed "to coincide with Aretha Franklin's 70th Birthday" (Rewind: The Aretha Franklin Songbook, released August 3, 2012).
- Missing / ambiguous facts blocking the question: Andy Yorke's birth date is not present in the passages (Missing fact: "Andy Yorke birth date"); Aretha Franklin's explicit birth date is not stated verbatim (bridge entity: Aretha Franklin; available clues only: "21-year-old" in 1963 and "70th Birthday" referenced in 2012).
- Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
  1. Fetch the "Aretha Franklin" article — inspect lede/infobox "Born" and "Early life"/"Biography" sections for exact birth date.  
  2. Fetch the "Andy Yorke" article — inspect lede/infobox "Born" (if ambiguous, fetch "Andy Yorke (disambiguation)").  
  3. If Andy Yorke ambiguous, search "Andy Yorke birth date" or fetch "Andy Yorke (musician)" page and inspect lede/infobox.  
  4. If needed, fetch reliable sources (e.g., Britannica or official biographies) for corroboration of both birth dates.
hop2_docs: Frank H. Warder | Frank H. Warder (birth date unknown - 1955) was the New York State Superintendent of Banks.  Prior to that, Warder was Secretary of the New York Bryan League.  He was convicted of taking a bribe and spent 3 and half years in prison Sing Sing.

Date of birth of Jesus | The date of birth of Jesus is not stated in the gospels or in any secular text, but most scholars assume a date of birth between 6 BC and 4 BC.  The historical evidence is too incomplete to allow a definitive dating, but the date is estimated through two different approaches—one by analyzing references to known historical events mentioned in the nativity accounts in the Gospels of Luke and Matthew, and the second by working backwards from the estimation of the start of the ministry of Jesus.
List of Presidents of the Philippines by date of birth | List of Presidents of the Philippines by date of birth

The Birth of Venus (Cabanel) | The Birth of Venus (French: "Naissance de Venus") is a painting by the French artist Alexandre Cabanel (1823–1889).  It was painted in 1863, and is now in the Musée d'Orsay in Paris.  A second and smaller version (85 x 135.9 cm) from ca. 1864 is in Dahesh Museum of Art.  A third (106 x 182.6 cm) version dates from 1875; it is in the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York City.
List of Prime Ministers of Thailand by date of birth | List of Prime Ministers of Thailand by date of birth
List of Vice Presidents of the Philippines by date of birth | List of Vice Presidents of the Philippines by date of birth

Whipple's index | Whipple's index (or index of concentration), invented by American demographer George Chandler Whipple (1866–1924), is a method to measure the tendency for individuals to inaccurately report their actual age or date of birth.  Respondents to a census or other survey sometimes report their age or date of birth as a round number (typically ending in 0 and 5), or to be more culturally favorable, for example, so that they appear younger or to have been born on a date considered luckier than their actual date of birth.
